issue: Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process

entries.position_summary: They regret the failure to adopt final documents in 2015 and 2022. While a final document is important, they prioritize preserving the NPT review process and avoiding actions that could cause its collapse.
entries.quote: We regret to note that the 2015 and 2022 Review Conferences failed to adopt final documents. At the same time, the adoption of a final document either at the 2026 Review Conference, or at this session of its Preparatory Committee is certainly an important, yet not the main task. To our mind, we should prioritize avoiding any actions that would lead to the collapse of the review process.

entries.position_summary: Advocates that the third PrepCom focus on agreeing modalities and procedural matters for the 2026 Review Conference to maximize time for substantive debates at the RevCon.
entries.quote: We suggest that the third session of the Preparatory Committee should focus on modalities and procedural matters of the 2026 Review Conference in order to enable the Conference itself to devote as much time as possible to substantive issues.

entries.position_summary: They urge the 11th NPT Review Conference to adopt outcome documents, emphasizing decision-making by consensus and consideration of each participant’s political priorities and constraints.
entries.quote: 5 We expect that we manage to agree on adopting outcome documents of the 11th NPT Review Conference. To do that, we need to act in the spirit of consensus and duly take into account the political priorities and limitations of each of the participants in the review process.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates that host states for NPT review meetings must issue visas promptly and free of charge to all participants, irrespective of national restrictions. They have proposed adding this requirement to the draft decision on strengthening the review process.
entries.quote: We believe it essential that the recommendations on the strengthening of the review process should envisage that the host Parties should issue visas for all participants in the NPT review meetings free of charge and as soon as possible, irrespective of any national restrictions. We have suggested the insertion of a pertinent point in the draft decision on strengthening the review process. We hope that it will be taken into account.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes PrepCom procedures that limit States Parties’ statements while granting a separate session to NGOs, asserting that States Parties should have priority over NGOs and other observers. It calls for this priority to be codified in recommendations on strengthening the review process.
entries.quote: Third. It has surprised us that at the Preparatory Committee meetings time limits apply to the statements of the NPT States parties while a separate session is given to the so called non-governmental organizations. It is our firm belief that States parties to the Treaty should have priority in expressing their views before both NGOs and any other observers. This also should be put down in the recommendations on the strengthening of the review process.

entries.position_summary: The country supports restoring the NPT review process to its normal schedule and commends the Chair and UN Secretariat for their efforts. It urges that the 2026 Review Conference be scheduled for April and May, mirroring the successful resolution of dates for the third PrepCom session.
entries.quote: We appreciate the efforts made by the Chairmanship in cooperation with the UN Secretariat in order to bring the work of the review process back within its usual schedule. We hope that the issue of holding the 2026 Review Conference in April and May will be resolved as successfully, as was that of the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee.

entries.position_summary: The country notes repeated failures to adopt outcome documents in recent review cycles but argues such documents, while important, are not essential for the NPT’s stability. It views the prolonged lack of consensus as a sign of negative trends that should be analyzed and addressed to strengthen the Treaty and improve future review processes.
entries.quote: At the same time, in the last two cycles of the Treaty, the parties did not manage to adopt an outcome document. In general, we do not intend to overdramatize the situation. We believe that the outcome document is an important element of the review cycle, but in principle not necessary, since its absence does not affect the stability of the NPT. However, the very fact that we have been unable to reach consensus for more than 12 years shows that the NPT community has accumulated certain negative trends, which we need to analyze and try to overcome, not only for the sake of the success of the review processes, but also in order to strengthen the Treaty.

entries.position_summary: The country strongly supports establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone as part of the 1995 NPT commitments, urging participation by all regional states, specifically calling on the United States and Israel to engage. It criticizes the United States for blocking the 2015 NPT Review Conference final document over language on the zone, signaling concern for the health of the NPT review process.
entries.quote: Certainly, this process can only be meaningful if it involves all Middle East countries excepting no one. In this context, we once again call on the United States, who co-sponsored the 1995 resolution, and Israel not to disregard the Conference, but rather take this opportunity to contribute their vision and engage in an open discussion on strengthening security in the Middle East. We emphasize that we regard the decision on establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East as an integral part of the arrangements concerning the NPT reached in 1995. We regret that the United States blocked the final document of the 2015 NPT Review Conference in order to prevent a language on the WMD-free zone.

entries.position_summary: The country insists that UN efforts should not replace the NPT process and that tensions over the Middle East must be addressed within the NPT framework ahead of the 2026 Review Conference. It reaffirms that the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East remains valid until fully implemented.
entries.quote: It is important to emphasize that the UN work is not a substitute for the NPT process. On the way to the 2026 NPT Review Conference, it will be critical to demonstrate that the United Nations forum addresses the tension around the Middle East issue in the context of the NPT review process. We underline once again that the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East remains in force until its full implementation.

entries.position_summary: The country urges a respectful, constructive NPT review process, warning against inserting unacceptable provisions into outcomes. It favors a lowest-common-denominator approach to secure adoption of an outcome document and notes that such a document, while important, is optional under the NPT.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, In conclusion, I would like to express our hope that the Preparatory Committee session will enable us to make progress in addressing the challenges facing the NPT and will also provide a good basis for the Review Conference. I would like to warn against repeating the mistakes of previous cycles, and to emphasize that, if we want to conduct the review process in a constructive manner, the parties to the Treaty should treat each other with respect and avoid imposing obviously unacceptable provisions in the outcome document. The only way to ensure the adoption of such a document is to prepare it according to the principle of the lowest common denominator. I would like to emphasize that it is an important, but optional, element of the NPT review.

entries.position_summary: The country urges that the NPT review process devote greater attention to peaceful uses of nuclear energy. It highlights its own deployment of advanced nuclear power technologies as a driver of social and economic development.
entries.quote: It is our firm belief that the review process should pay more attention to the matters of the peaceful uses of nuclear energy. Our States are a vivid example of how the creation of nuclear power generating capacities based on advanced technologies contributes to social and economic progress.

entries.position_summary: Russia affirms constructive participation in the 2023 Working Group on strengthening the NPT review process and expresses commitment to developing concrete recommendations ahead of the 2026 Review Conference PrepCom.
entries.quote: STATEMENT by the Deputy Head of the Delegation of the Russian Federation at the Second Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, Deputy Director of the Department for Non-Proliferation and Arms Control of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Russian Federation Mr. Mikhail Kondratenkov (Cluster III Specific Issue on Strengthening the Review Process of the NPT) (Geneva, 30 July 2024) Mr. Chairman, Russia constructively participated in the activities of the Working Group on further strengthening the review process of the NPT (Vienna, 24-28 July 2023) on the eve of the first session of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 NPT Review Conference and was committed to elaborating specific recommendations.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that decisions not adopted by consensus are non-binding on states that did not support them and cautions that misuse of voting procedures could erode confidence and discourage states from participating in the NPT review process.
entries.quote: Any non-consensus decisions will remain non-binding for the States that have not supported them. Furthermore, abusing the voting procedure will most likely make a number of States doubt whether to continue participating in the review process.

entries.position_summary: Russia expresses appreciation to the UN Secretariat and UNOG for organizing the NPT Preparatory Committee session, signaling support for the NPT review process infrastructure and its proceedings.
entries.quote: Russian delegation takes this opportunity to commend the United Nations Secretariat and the United Nations Office at Geneva for organizing the Second Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT).

entries.position_summary: The country supports strengthening gender equality and gender mainstreaming within the NPT, particularly in the review process, and endorses achieving gender balance. It aligns with the Preparatory Committee’s emphasis on these priorities.
entries.quote: Recently, many States Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons have emphasized the importance of strengthening gender equality and gender mainstreaming in the Treaty’s review process. The session of the Preparatory Committee for the 2024 Review Conference made strong reference to the need for gender balance.

entries.position_summary: The country views the UN Conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone as making steady, constructive progress toward outlining a future treaty and remains open to involving states not yet participating. It considers these developments beneficial both within the UN framework and for advancing the issue through the NPT review process.
entries.quote: 13. With regard to the substantive aspects of the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone, the negotiation process is ongoing. Despite the unprecedented escalation in the region, the States represented at the forum are not closing the door to the possible involvement of countries not yet participating. The discussions indicate that the Parties are gradually approaching a discussion on the outlines of a future zone treaty. This is beneficial in advancing the establishment of a WMD-free zone, both at the United Nations and through the review process.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes efforts to use the NPT review process to impose transparency and accountability measures on nuclear-weapon States, describing this as coercive and counterproductive. It blames certain states’ narrow, last-minute, transparency-focused proposals for blocking consensus and argues for a broader, consensus-based approach to strengthening the review process.
entries.quote: However, the purpose of strengthening the NPT review process turned out to be merely declaratory, while the main task was to impose transparency and accountability measures on nuclear-weapon States and to convert the NPT review process into a means of oversight and coercion. The Working Group failed to deliver recommendations adopted by consensus due to the narrow and maximalist approach of certain countries, who, instead of agreeing on a wide range of recommendations to strengthen the NPT review process, opted to wait till the last minute to table proposals concerning exclusively the matters of transparency and accountability of nuclear-weapon States and tried to impose them on the rest of the members of the Group as an outcome. However, that did not work. It is noteworthy that those who prevented the adoption of recommendations reflecting the lowest common denominator included the countries that had initiated the establishment of the Group. This only proves that not every delegation pursued the strengthening the NPT review process as their main objective. As a result, the members of the Group only wasted their time, efforts and resources.

entries.position_summary: Russia asserts it provides transparency on NPT implementation within nationally determined limits and supports the existing 2013 P5 common reporting framework without changes. It emphasizes that each nuclear-weapon State should set its own acceptable transparency boundaries based on strategic realities and national security interests.
entries.quote: As for Russia's transparency measures, we have continuously and within admissible limits informed the international community of our steps to implement our NPT obligations. As part of the Treaty review process Russia, like other N5 countries, submits national reports prepared according to the agreed common reporting framework. The reporting framework agreed by the N5 in 2013 remains relevant and needs no adjustments at the moment. Each nuclear-weapon State can independently determine acceptable transparency limits depending on the strategic realities and its national security interests.

entries.position_summary: The country highlights divisions within the UN GGE on nuclear disarmament verification (NDV), arguing against developing verification procedures early and in isolation from comprehensive disarmament negotiations. It notes some consensus on NDV principles but expects limited additional contributions to this topic in the NPT review process in the near term.
entries.quote: Discussions in the specialized UN Group of Governmental Experts confirmed the lack of unity in relation to approaches to NDV and its scope. Most importantly, there is no agreement on the advisability of the "early" development of verification procedures in isolation from comprehensive negotiation work that will be required in the future to conclude nuclear disarmament agreements. All of this range of views is presented in the final report of the Group, which also contains a number of important consensus statements on the fundamental principles on which the NDV should be based. It is unlikely that in the foreseeable future the NPT community will have much to add on this topic for the purposes of the Treaty review process.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that intergovernmental associations (e.g., the EU) should only speak after NPT States Parties at review meetings, unless represented by NPT States Parties, and that UNGA resolutions are irrelevant to this procedural issue. They have proposed language in the draft decision on strengthening the review process to prevent perceived discrimination and EU advantages.
entries.quote: 2 We believe that this should be put to rights to eliminate discrimination resulting from giving the EU advantages over other inter-State associations. Any references to the UN General Assembly resolution are irrelevant in this case. The Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committees' sessions have their own rules of procedure. Therefore, it should be clearly stipulated that at NPT review meetings the representatives of intergovernmental associations can only be given the floor after the States parties have made their statements. Exceptions are only made if such associations are represented by the NPT State parties. We have proposed a pertinent insertion in draft decision on the strengthening of the review process. We hope that it will be taken into account.

entries.position_summary: The country calls for a more effective NPT review process grounded in professional, committed dialogue among States Parties. It advocates addressing even contentious issues through this forum to overcome differences and build future work on the basis of consensus.
entries.quote: In our view, the discussions at the 10th Review Conference clearly demonstrated that a professional and committed conversation among States parties to the Treaty is needed on the NPT platform. This approach should also be followed when discussing the issues inducing the most heated debates. This is the only acceptable means of overcoming differences and finding common ground that would allow us to build further work on the basis of consensus.

entries.position_summary: The country rejects immediate outlawing of nuclear weapons and artificial disarmament deadlines, arguing these ignore strategic realities, the security interests of nuclear-weapon states, and the principle of undiminished security. It insists disarmament must occur in the context of general and complete disarmament and warns that non-consensus initiatives like the TPNW undermine consensus and the NPT forum's sustainability.
entries.quote: 3 We believe that any ideas of a 'short cut' to 'nuclear zero' through immediate and unconditional renunciation and outlawing of nuclear weapons are unworkable and counter-productive. So are any attempts to set artificial deadlines for disarmament, especially in the current highly turbulent and unpredictable international environment. Such ideas fail to take into account the politico-military and strategic realities, disregard the security interests of nuclear-weapon States, run counter to the principle of undiminished security and are set outside the context of general and complete disarmament. Attempts to push forward such non-consensus initiatives only exacerbate the contradictions at the NPT forum, which would be detrimental to the sustainability of the nuclear non-proliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the NPT review process procedures, calling it unacceptable that the European Union, represented by Brussels officials, delivers statements before most states at review and PrepCom sessions. It implies that speaking order and participation should prioritize states over regional organizations.
entries.quote: Second. During both the previous review process events and the current Preparatory Committee session we have encountered an absolutely unacceptable situation when the European Union represented by its Brussels officials has not been merely making statement equally with States, but making it before most of them.

entries.position_summary: The country calls for a realistic, mutually acceptable, and integrated approach to disarmament that balances all parties’ interests under Article VI. It opposes imposing unilateral positions in review documents and stresses consensus-based decision-making to ensure successful NPT review outcomes.
entries.quote: 2 The disarmament undertakings that are to contribute to the promotion of the relevant NPT objectives in practice, should be based both on a realistic perception of the international security environment, and on elements that either have been generally agreed upon or can be mutually acceptable. In other words, they would naturally build on the integrated approach to disarmament aiming to maintain the balance of interests of all parties to the Treaty. They would also involve renunciation of attempts to imperiously and unconditionally enforce one's view as the only correct one in order to put it down in the review process documents, which can only reinforce the trend towards the failure to adopt consensus decisions at the review cycle meetings, and consequently should be prevented.

entries.position_summary: Russia expresses support for the NPT review process, congratulates the chair, pledges constructive engagement, and commends the UN Secretariat and UNODA for organizing the PrepCom. The stance is supportive of effective review mechanisms and process management.
entries.quote: Unofficial translation STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Federation at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, General debate (New York, 29 April 2025) Mr. Chair, We congratulate you on your election to this high office and assure you that we stand ready to constructively engage with you on all issues that arise in the course of the review process. We commend the United Nations Secretariat and the United Nations Office for Disarmament Affairs for organizing the third session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT).

entries.position_summary: The country pledges constructive, respectful, and depoliticized engagement with all delegations during the third PrepCom, signaling support for an effective and inclusive NPT review process.
entries.quote: We are committed to constructive engagement with all delegations during the third Session of the Preparatory Committee, in a spirit of mutual respect without politicization

entries.position_summary: The delegation notes the lapse of the Working Group’s mandate and favors addressing review-process matters—including universality and withdrawal—within Cluster 3 as before. It argues that strengthening the review process should not focus solely on nuclear-weapon States’ transparency; transparency and confidence-building are means to advance arms control, disarmament, and non-proliferation. Citing Article VI, it stresses that nuclear disarmament sits within general and complete disarmament and that both nuclear-weapon and non-nuclear-weapon States should report on implementing their disarmament obligations.
entries.quote: 2 The mandate of the Working Group on further strengthening the review process of the Treaty expired, and there has been no decision to extend it. Now these matters can be discussed exclusively as a cluster 3-specific issue among other issues, which include the NPT universalization, withdrawal from the Treaty, etc., as they were discussed before. In addition, the deliberations on the strengthening of the NPT review process should not be confined to discussing the nuclear-weapon States' transparency and accountability. Transparency and other confidence- building measures are not an end in themselves, but rather a means of addressing arms control, disarmament and non-proliferation challenges. What is more, it should be remembered that Article VI of the NPT puts nuclear disarmament within the context of the general and complete disarmament. Hence, both nuclear-weapon and non-nuclear-weapon States should report on the implementation of their disarmament obligations.

entries.position_summary: The country appreciates that the U.S. issued visas on time for most of its delegation but notes two experts received visas only at the last minute, and only after the Chair intervened. It implies that such delays hinder effective participation and that host-country visa practices should improve to support a robust NPT review process.
entries.quote: First. We cannot but recognize that this year the United States has issued visas for the majority of the members of our delegations in a timely manner, and we express our appreciation for that. However, two of our experts had difficulties. Their visas were issued a day or two before the beginning of the event. What is more, that happened only after the Chair had intervened.

entries.position_summary: The country insists that only consensus-approved recommendations should guide decisions in the current NPT review cycle. It argues that outcomes on a Cluster 3 (peaceful uses) issue should be considered alongside other proposals at the 2026 Review Conference and assessed for their impact on the NPT review process and the Treaty overall.
entries.quote: We believe that recommendations that have not been approved by consensus cannot be viewed as a workable basis for decisions to be adopted within the current NPT review cycle. The outcome of discussions under cluster 3-specific issue should be considered together with the rest of the proposals that will be tabled at the 2026 Review Conference and assessed, inter alia, in terms of their influence on the Treaty review process and the NPT in general.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates for staff from the IAEA, OPCW, and the BWC Implementation Support Unit to attend and play a more prominent role in the NPT Conference, promoting cross-regime engagement. This aims to strengthen the NPT review process and leverage IAEA expertise.
entries.quote: We also consider it important that the staff of the IAEA and OPCW Secretariats, as well as the BWC Implementation Support Unit, attend the Conference and participate more prominently in it in the future.

entries.position_summary: Russia asserts that deep and growing disagreements among States Parties on nuclear disarmament—worsened by current international tensions and divergent security perceptions—are obstructing practical progress under the NPT’s disarmament pillar. It highlights ideological and conceptual divides that hinder consensus as the review cycle concludes.
entries.quote: Unofficial translation STATEMENT by the Deputy Head of the Delegation of the Russian Federation at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (Cluster I: Nuclear disarmament) New York, 1 May 2025 Mr. Chair, We have to note with regret that as we are drawing closer to the completion of the current NPT review cycle, we observe no indications that the NPT forum is to overcome the deep contradictions concerning the nuclear disarmament pillar of the Treaty. Alongside with that, we experience considerable negative impact of the current atmosphere of high tension and conflict in the international affairs, which makes an increasing number of States feel dissatisfied with the regional and global security situation, as well as the level of their own protection. Differing significantly in both their national circumstances and their perceptions of the causes that have provoked the current deplorable situation, countries often come to diametrically opposite conclusions and have diametrically opposite ambitions as regards their national priorities at multilateral fora, including the NPT. As a result, the States parties' basic disagreements on practical disarmament matters are additionally exacerbated by their fundamental ideological and conceptual contradictions concerning approaches to the maintenance of international security and stability.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that NATO states politicize the NPT by asserting unilateral interpretations to pressure others, which undermines the review process. It criticizes the 2015 and 2022 review dynamics, including the exclusion of Middle East WMDFZ language, and appears to support stronger, depoliticized treatment of the Middle East zone in outcomes.
entries.quote: The excessive politicization of work within the NPT should be noted. In recent decades, a certain group of countries - more precisely, the member States of the NATO nuclear bloc and their allies - have usurped the right to interpret States' compliance with their obligations under the Treaty and have used its provisions to pressure undesired States and justify interference in their internal affairs. This approach has also had a negative impact on the review process: during the 2015 Conference, some NPT parties deemed passages on a Middle East Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone inappropriate for an outcome document; and in 2022, a group of States found it possible to promote controversial and obviously
